[IMAGE:0]It's just shy of midnight, so here we go with a new Magic Monday. The picture is of Michael Maier, one of the great alchemists of the early 17th century and a major figure in the early Rosicrucian movement. His best-known alchemical books combine prose, verse, vivid copperplate engravings, and (in some cases) musical compositions, all hinting at the cryptic dimensions of the alchemist's art. 

Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. Any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.0 of The Magic Monday FAQ here.

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use the button below to access my online tip jar. If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here.

With that said, have at it!

It's the porn addict guy again. I've got a quick update on where I'm at and my plan for the next few weeks, as well as some questions. I've been doing the SoP daily, first thing in the morning, for almost two weeks now, and it feels awesome. It has helped with reducing my porn use, and I feel much better overall. I feel more calm and pure, in a way which is hard to describe. The effects are most powerful right after I do it, lasting for between 5-30 minutes (it seems to vary from day to day almost chaotically), and then fading over the course of a couple hours. However, some residual effects last for the whole day now.

I'm up to calling air, and I can feel it respond quite strongly. Some days are better than others, but this morning I almost felt like I was swept off my feet with it. I assume I'll get used to it, but it seems to work very well for me!

It's also had three very unexpected side effects, both of which I think will be beneficial in the long run. The first is that my cell phone is now painful to use. This started on the first day, and it has turned into a cycle: the less I use my phone, the more I notice it feels horrible. Thus, I'm planning to reduce how much I use it for the next few weeks, and then hopefully ditch it entirely.

The second is that the internet seems dramatically more boring, enough I don't want to touch it at all while the SoP effects are at their strongest. This hasn't yet had much of an effect beyond me realizing my entire life is fairly lame right now, but it has motivated me to start looking for things to do. My plan is to start doing things which I need to do (such as housework), and gradually start more things in over time.

The third is that fast food has started tasting worse. This is most noticeable with one chain in particular, which before I would feel tempted to eat at every time I passed, but now I find borderline inedible. This caught me by surprise, and it's leading to some fairly drastic changes in how I eat, not so much because I'm trying, but because the way I used to eat is increasingly striking me as gross.

1) Am I correct to think that cell phones, and EM radiation in general are etheric pollution, and that it makes sense to minimize use as much as possible for maintaining health on the etheric level? As I practice, will it become easier to tell what devices produce enough radiation to be dangerous?

2) The internet becoming boring was an unexpected side effect. Is this likely because of me becoming more resistant to the malevolent entities which use it? What strikes me about it is that this seems to imply that the internet being addictive is largely a result of malevolent entities, and this has some disturbing implications for what the efforts that the tech companies have made to push it in that direction have done....

3) The fast food thing also strikes me as odd. My current guess is that as I pay more attention to the inner planes, the fact that fast food isn't really food for anything past the physical body is becoming more noticeable. Is this plausible, or do you think there's more to it?

4) I've managed to completely eliminate porn use in the morning. Evenings and the night are still problematic, but I've reduced it by around 50% already, as one of the times I used to use it was shortly after waking up, and that's no longer tempting. I was expecting to see it take quite a bit longer to see major changes, but already things have gotten quite a bit better. Is my experience typical?

5) When I finish the SoP I sometimes have a bit of a headache and a slight pain at my solar plexus. Both of them vanish within a few minutes, but do you know what causes them?

I've also made a few plans for things to add in. The first is starting the process of learning meditation starting Tuesday, when I add in the next element to the SoP. The other is cold showers, as I've looked into the claimed benefits and think it looks quite promising, both in terms of ordinary benefits and reducing porn use.

6) One of the weirder things I've seen with cold showers is this notion that cold showers clear blockages with the lymphatic system. For some reason I want to think there's a connection between the etheric level and the lymphatic system, but I'm unable to say why. Do you think this is plausible? Also, would this then mean cold showers have an etheric effect?

Finally, I'm going to be visiting family over Christmas, and will be completely cutting myself off from the internet for the trip. This will get me close to two weeks where it'll be impossible for me to use porn: all I need to do is not bring my phone or computer.

As I will have no internet access until January, I'd like to take the time now to wish you a Happy Solstice, Merry Christmas and Happy New Year!

comments.title: LotR Musings
comments.content:
Hey JMG,

My wife and I are reading through The Lord of the Rings and we recently read this bit from The Two Towers:

“Hope is not victory. War is upon us and all our friends, a war in which only the use of the Ring could give us surety of victory. It fills me with great sorrow and great fear: for much shall be destroyed and all may be lost. I am Gandalf, Gandalf the White, but Black is mightier still.”

This passage surprised me because usually the forces of light are depicted as being inherently stronger (if only just) than the forces of darkness. Furthermore, it got me thinking about magic and our own world. I typically hold a pretty practical approach to magic, but now I’m wondering if there is some magical Sauron-like power I ought to be fighting against, or if I’m in a sort of war without really knowing it. Put differently, I wonder if there is a higher magical calling that I’m unknowingly not participating in. Do you see any correlations between what Gandalf says here and our own reality? If so, what might be the “Black” in our world and is it indeed “mightier still” than magic of a lighter nature? Or are these just overblown musings on fiction?

Thanks!

replies.title: Re: LotR Musings
replies.content: As usual with Tolkien, there's a lot of occult wisdom there. No, there's no Dark Lord you ought to be fighting, except in a metaphorical sense -- but there's an immense burden of misery and ignorance and wrong in this world, in comparison with which the strength and wisdom and love that we can bring to bear individually is pretty weak. Yes, there is a higher magical calling; whether or not you should be participating in it is the great choice you, and only you, can make; Dion Fortune, who's a useful guide in this, talks about how the initiate attunes his or her consciousness to higher realms of being and manifest the qualities of those realms in this one, and helps transform the world. That's Gandalf's job, too -- and the job of everyone who faces the same challenge, to stand or fall under the Shadow.

replies.content: Not the original poster, but as a follow up question, which Dion Fortune book(s) are you referring to here?

replies.title: Re: LotR Musings
replies.content: I'd have to do digging -- for about a decade I read part of one of her essays just before going to bed every night, and I don't always remember which essay was the source of which concept! If I had to guess, though, it would be The Training and Work of an Initiate. (Or just possibly The Circuit of Force.)

replies.title: Re: LotR Musings
replies.content:
Thanks! That’s helpful.

I recognize that the origin of evil is a hairy subject, but, in your simplified opinion, where does the Shadow originate from? Why are humans in the unique position to stand or fall under it? Is it in part because we’re on a sort of reincarnation threshold between this world and whatever’s next?

replies.title: Re: LotR Musings
replies.content: Yes, in part. In the technical language of occultism, we're in the transitional phase between astral and mental existence; we have mental sheaths that have not yet become mental bodies, and that places us in a condition where imbalance is very easy. It's also a matter of occult tradition that certain events very early in the history of our species brought us into an inappropriate mode of contact with a certain category of malign spirit; this is what's behind the allegory of the Fall in the book of Genesis, and comparable events in a great many other mythologies.

comments.title: Cooking
comments.content:
From my observations, the nourishing qualities of food are profoundly affected by the energy of the person preparing it. I’m convinced there are big-time etheric differences between fast food and homemade food, and of course fast food is vastly inferior. The more deeply involved a person was with the process of making the dish, the more restoring/healing energy the dish possesses. That’s why the food prepared by the awesome lady who knows you by name at the Mexican hole-in-the-wall restaurant is better than the hastily thrown together burrito at Taco Bell, and why the burrito is superior to its frozen, microwavable counterpart. If it’s grown in your own garden, for instance a salad with your own lettuce, radishes, onion, and tomato with homemade salad dressing, it’s incredibly restorative, meaning it will knock the flu you were about to get right out of you or bring you out of a foul mood. A salad from Panera or whatever just would not do the same thing. Additionally, the energy of the person making the food combines with the food’s energy and that is a significant part of what makes it genuinely nourishing. If you’ve ever run into that person who follows an easy recipe and the food is always off or foul, I’m convinced it’s an etheric phenomenon. (Maybe they could try to fix it by appealing to some kitchen gods?)

At any rate, that’s why I think every human should learn to cook at least a few basic hot and cold dishes and snacks, but especially magically-inclined humans. Cooking is its own crude form of alchemy.

Wishing JMG and you all a beautiful, serene, and fulfilling Solstice.

To understand homeopathy you need to know about the etheric plane, the plane of life force "next to" the material plane. The process of succussion transfers the etheric substance of the remedy (called "simple substance" in John Tyler Kent's writings on homeopathy) to the lactose base. The result is a kind of biochemical judo.

Sodium sulphate, for example, will give you nausea and diarrhea if you gulp down a bunch of it dissolved in water. Use it to make the homeopathic remedy Nat. sulph. 6x, though, and you've got lactose pills that have the etheric substance of sodium sulphate but only 1 ppm of the mineral itself. You take a dose, and your etheric body detects the etheric substance and, in effect, warns your digestive tract, "here comes a whole bunch of sodium sulphate!" Your body, which always tries to maintain its equilibrium, makes biochemical adjustments to counteract the sodium sulphate before it hits -- and then no sodium sulphate worth mentioning shows up.

If you've already got the symptoms you'd get by ingesting a bunch of sodium sulphate, and you use that effect, fairly often that bit of biochemical judo will make the neusea and diarrhea go away. That's why a homeopathic remedy treats the symptoms that the crude raw material for the remedy causes.

2. It started off with specific mantras and prayers to Hindu gods that I was taught and over time evolved to other Hindu gods, and then to praying to all gods together and then to one supreme god who combined all the others and now to a divine deity or "force" that I have no name for and no idea who or what it is. None of this was planned, it's just kind of small changes in the nightly prayer over decades has ended up here.

I don't even know how to relate to the deity I'm praying to now, or how to deal with him or name him. How would I add someone like that to the SOP?

replies.title: Re: SOP, Visualization etc
replies.content:
1) For the Hindu pantheon, the pattern depends on whether your family has a connection to Vishnu and/or his avataras, on the one hand, or to Shiva and/or some form of Devi, on the other. In either case the first four are the Lokapalas: Indra to the east for air, Varuna to the west for water, Yama (the all-devouring) to the south for fire, and Kubera to the north for earth. Spirit Below should be assigned to whichever goddess is most relevant to your family traditions: Lakshmi if your family reveres Vishnu, Parvati if your family reveres Shiva, and so on. Spirit Above is assigned to the corresponding god.

2) Don't worry about adding such a god to the SoP, unless he specifically gives you a name and an image to use. Simply keep praying to him, and let him guide you as he chooses

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) The Sphere of Protection works primarily with astral forces -- that's true of any magical working that relies on will and imagination. The DMH system, though, is oriented toward using mastery of the astral as a basis for work with the etheric. The SoP and the Work of the Grail given in the DMH also develops an alignment of lower and higher self. Like most systems of magic, the DMH system has more than one effect!

2) No, because you come to the experience of scrying with a different cultural and linguistic background than the person who wrote the Mabinogion. Furthermore, everything in the Mabinogion is symbolic, not literal -- it's an extraordinarily rich and complex set of teaching stories that takes years of meditation to unpack, and I'm convinced that was the intention of its author. Don't worry about it; let yourself perceive what you perceive. You might, though, consider asking the beings you encounter in your scrying for their advice on making sense of the Mabinogion?

3) There's no such thing as doing a course of magical study too slowly. There is such a thing as rushing through it too quickly. Take the time you need to do it thoroughly, and don't worry about how long it takes others.

The difficulty here is that the universe doesn't just consist of material and spiritual worlds; there are realms between those two. Science is the appropriate way to understand things of the material world, and religion is the appropriate way to understand things of the spiritual world...but the world between these, the world that occultists divide into etheric, astral, and mental planes, is not well addressed by either science or religion.

That's where occultism comes into its own. replies.content:
It's worth noting that the church makes sacramentals available to the laity, and that they can be used for this purpose.

As far as I know, there would be absolutely nothing wrong with a banishing ritual that included some combination of the following--

1. Sign of the Cross. If this is done slowly, reverently, and in Latin, so much the better. And is there anything at all wrong with visualizing light descending from heaven into the space, and then extending infinitely in either direction? This could be understood as a prayer, but in images, rather than in words.

2. Pray the Asperges Me, and then sprinkle holy water to the four directions.

3. Take blessed incense and wave it to the four directions with a suitable prayer. (Perhaps, as JMG gives in one of his books, "The voice of the Lord is powerful, the voice of the Lord is full of majesty, the voice of hte Lord divideth the flames of the fire.)

4. As an additional option, a bell may be exorcised and blessed, and rung three times (for the holy trinity) or seven times (for any of the various sets of seven). Church bells were historically used for exactly this purpose-- clearing out evil spirits.

5. Speaking of things used for this purpose, you can also place blessed salt into the corner of the room you use for prayer. Is this a pagan custom? Hardly; you can find details on its use at the (very traditional) Fisheaters website here: https://www.fisheaters.com/salt.html "Because of its exorcism and blessing, it is a powerful sacramental in keeping away demons. To obtain blessed salt, just take ordinary salt to your priest and ask him to bless it."

6. And of course there is the use of candles, which can also be exorcised and blessed, and anointed with consecrated oils.

7. The Saint Patrick's breastplate would be a suitable prayer to use at this point, given that it explicitly relates to the protection of an individual and his space; the full thing is given here: https://www.ourcatholicprayers.com/st-patricks-breastplate.html

From an occult perspective, all these practices work at the interface between the physical and spiritual worlds. From a traditional Christian perspective, they also work at the interface of the physical and spiritual worlds. It personally doesn't make any sense to me that many Christians think they're forbidden from believing in layers of reality between the physical and the spiritual. Even if one did hold that belief, though, the various elements given here-- salt, water, sound, fire, incense, prayer-- would nevertheless have their intended effect on the space. The tool of discursive meditation, meanwhile, gives the Christian the ability to test the effect of these sacramentals on the "mental atmosphere" of the space. Is it easier to maintain focus, easier to think of holy things, easier to feel a sense of divine connection, and easier to avoid sin in the space thus prepared? Does the space start to take on a sense of holiness, so that just entering into it puts you into a spiritual frame of mind-- and even people who don't know you're doing it start to react similarly? Then it's working.

replies.title: Re: Integrating Practices
replies.content: In my experience, it's rarely a good idea to do more than one or, at most, two systems at a time. When I did my original Golden Dawn studies, for most of twenty years it was all Golden Dawn all the time. Then I got into Druidry via the Order of Bards Ovates and Druids (OBOD) and focused on that pretty exclusively until I'd finished the study course. Each time I picked up a course of study, if it had what I consider a full range of magical practices, I focused on it, completed it, and then went on. If it didn't, I found something else more or less compatible to do while working on it -- for example, I was practicing AODA-style Druid magic, meditation, and spirituality while I did hoodoo, radionics, and spagyric alchemy (one of those at a time, not all at once!) Now and then I'll cycle back through a course I've already done, when I decide that will be useful, and I do it on the same basis I did it originally. So there's always a modest number of practices to be done each day, but which ones I work with vary every year or three.

replies.title: Re: Integrating Practices

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
The magical tradition the AODA shares with several other (now mostly inactive) orders doesn't put much time into discussing the upper end of the Path, for good reason. One of the great mistakes of the Golden Dawn tradition was that it did so at great length; the result was that ever since there's been a steady stream of individuals who convinced themselves that they'd achieved a much higher state than they had, and they pretty reliably turned into emotionally unstable egomaniacs who basically couldn't take a step without tripping over their own dicks -- oh, good afternoon, Aleister! We were just talking about you.

As Dion Fortune noted in one of her books, it takes three lifetimes of hard work just to get to the point of becoming fully initiated; the Abyss is a long way beyond that, and quite a few writers have suggested that nobody gets there while still in a human body. The focus of AODA and its sister orders is on teaching people to lay the foundations of their own magical and spiritual path and proceed on it, so speculations about the far end of the path tend to be downplayed.

comments.title: About Lemuria
comments.content:
Dear JMG:

I have a couple of related questions for you this week.

1) What place (if any) does the concept of Lemuria still play in the theory and practice of Western esotericism? Wendy Berg, of the Dion Fortune lineage, in her books "Polarity Magic: the Secret History of Western Religion" and "Red Tree, White Tree: Faeries and Humans in Partnership" seems to equate the Age of Lemuria as a pre-Fall/pre-Atlantean higher state of being and its inhabitants synonymous with the denizens of Faerie and the Beni Elohim. Does this theory grok with you, or does an alternate theory satisfy you?

2) Are you familiar with the Lemurian Fellowship? I'd have to describe the organization as a synthesis of New Age Protestantism and some Blavatskian Theosophy. I'm not a member, nor feel called to take up its lessons course. Having said that, from what I've observed through its website and media, I do admire the practices they encourage: ethical living, cooperation and service to humanity through one's illumination, civic responsibility, right relationship with Nature and her laws, craftsmanship to create beautiful and useful items from natural materials (i.e. wood, stone, metal), etc. I'm not fond of their millenarian emphasis on creating a New Age Civilization, and a literal continent of Mu in the Pacific is a hard sell for me; but on the whole they seem more grounded and less "detached" then some other New Age organizations I've seen.

Thank you in advance for your feedback, it's always appreciated.

Sincerely,
Anon.

replies.title: Re: About Lemuria
replies.content:
1) As I see it, Lemuria was certainly pre-Atlantean but it wasn't pre-Fall. To the best of my understanding, what we call Lemuria is a dim memory, enhanced with scraps of inner plane experience, of an age of human civilization during the peak of the Ice Age, centered on the land mass that geologists now call Sundaland. Some legacies of the Lemurian civilization can still be traced around the Indian Ocean littoral and in east Asia.

I gather from surviving hints and scraps that the civilization was pretty impressive, but I don't accept the idea that it was Utopia; certainly Dion Fortune herself would have disagreed with that, as her comments in several books make clear. One other thing -- that first book you cited, Polarity Magic by Berg and Mike Harris, is notorious in magical circles these days, and not in a good way. I emphatically don't recommend it to anybody.

2) Good heavens, I had no idea that they were still around! They had a pretty good reputation back in the day. Yes, it's Christian Theosophy plus a good dollup of New Thought and another healthy serving of standard early 20th century American occultism: all things considered, not a bad blend at all.

I like to hike barefoot in the mountains near my house, up a pristine creek valley to visit a giant poplar, the biggest tree in Georgia I believe, nearly 7' in diameter at 4-man hug level. While Im focusing my breath entering meditation I've taken to visualizing myself on that trail, as if it is what's under the canopy in the Fortuna Major image, making my way upstream to an invented spring pool that is the source of the rushing river in the image. There I settle into a discursive position on a chair-height rock at the water's edge, pull the image I'm meditating on out of my head with the tip of a wand, and cast it out into the pool, where it fills the entire pool's surface. (Yes, rather like Dumbledore and his pensieve, I'm afraid...) Once it's there I usually magnify the part of the card I'm focusing on and get to it.

Meanwhile, on the physical plane, I have a beautiful maybe 1" flattened sphere of clear obsidian (an excellent scrying stone) sitting on the corresponding part of the physical card, magnifying it and drawing out its secrets.

As a package it's really becoming quite something! Meditation has since jumped sharply in clarity, focus, and my ability to unpack hidden meaning.

Then last week I finally got around to casting a geomantic chart to find out what planet rules my guardian genius. Surprised me, it's Saturn, not the Sun. And if there was any doubt about Carcer in the 11th, he had Tristitia with him in company demi-simple in the 12th. That kind of took me by surprise. And to be honest, Saturn scares the bejesus out of me.

The following night I had a long drawn-out dream, the only one of the night and it seemed to just keep running the whole time, of the planets Jupiter and Saturn floating in space, getting closer to each other, mixed with giant clocks and gears turning ever so slowly. Very Grand Mutation-esque! And the tone of the dream was cold, and remote, and stoic, and intimidating. Maybe even belittling.

In meditation the next morning (Saturday) I took a day off of the Fool's Journey (I was, and still am, on the Jupiter card, the Wheel of Fortune) to explore the dream a little further. And while I didn't get a lot in the way of explanation of the dream itself, I got the distinct impression that the image of Fortuna Major was something like a parting gift from the Sun, which has pretty much ruled my life up till now. Sort of like a homey space suit for my forthcoming journey out to the distant rings of Saturn, so far outside of my warm solar comfort zone. Made me feel like I can do this, like I could be ready for a new adventure, zipping off into such hostile territory, trying to warm up to a cold new master.

And I just wanted to share all that with whoever wanted to read it, because it's all been pretty breathtaking for me, these last 2 weeks (and here I was wondering if the 6 month mark had any significance...).

And I appreciate the space in which to do so, kind and benevolent host. Many thanks!

Tripp

comments.title: Les Druides
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

a) I’ve recently read a few books about ancient Celts and druids, their folklore and mythology, including a 1961 book Les Druides by Françoise Le Roux. I’ve made several conclusions from this:

1. Life of the ancient Celts was anything but idyllic, even in the absence of the Roman Empire. If we judge by both myth and history there was constant warfare between tribes, misery, truly barbaric customs, people were buried alive (“burial alive, it's such a stuffy death!” to borrow a phrase from Gilbert & Sullivan). And the Druids, they participated with all of it with glee, well maybe without glee, but they did. So many stories revolve around a druid putting a curse on somebody…I guess I’m trying to say that I became disenchanted with the Celts. I wonder if Celtic Reconstructionists know about all this stuff and if they do, why do they want to reconstruct it?

2. It is very uncertain if the iron age druids believed in metempsychosis, because they were the priests of the Celts and the Celts believed the deceased continued their lives practically unchanged in the land of the dead, so death was just a midpoint in a very long existence. Which is why the Celts had a custom of burying their warriors in full gear facing the direction where their enemies come from, so they could continue their fighting even after death. Some characters from Celtic myths do get to be reborn and live another life, but it was an exception rather than norm. Reincarnation may have existed as part of the esoteric Druid teaching, but we will never know.

3. Reading the stories about all the miracles conjured by druids and bards made me remember your words about how you believe that magic was stronger in the past. My take on it is that people used to be more sensitive to the higher planes of existence in the past and so things that were happening on the astral plane were as real and palpable to them as physical things.

replies.title: Re: Les Druides
replies.content:
I'm reading "The Boy Who Harnessed the Wind" currently, for a book club discussion I'm leading at the local library on it next month. Even in the modern era there is magic that is much more...stunning than the gentle stuff we're doing, at least as apprentice magicians in the West. Of course, I have no idea how intense our host's magic is, or that of his peers. Mine has gotten significantly more interesting in the 6 or 7 months I've been practicing; I can only imagine what it'll be like 6 or 7 YEARS from now!

In the book the author tells a story his grandfather swears to be true. He was walking in the jungle in Malawi one day when he came across a man who had just been bitten on the head by a cobra as he walked under the tree the cobra was in. The man was turning ashen gray and on his way out as the author's grandfather ran to the nearest village to fetch their witch doctor. The witch doctor came back with him immediately to find the man already dead, but put his foot on the man's chest and tossed an herbal concoction into the jungle around them. Almost immediately more than a hundred cobras slithered out of the jungle into the clearing around the small group, entranced by the wizard. He then crouched down on the dead man, both feet on his chest, and started chanting. The dead man sputtered and came to, complaining about the other man on his chest. Then the two of them together examined the fangs of every cobra in attendance to find out which one bit him. When they found the culprit the once-dead man forgave the cobra and let him live, then walked home for dinner.

The author swears this is a true story. And this would have happened since 1950. If so, one can only imagine what is possible in the realm of magic. Not that I'm interested in commanding a legion of cobras, but I'm sure you get the point...

Tripp

replies.title: Re: Les Druides
replies.content:

Obviously I adore Magic Monday for JMG's wit and wisdom, but I look forward to reading your insightful questions and stories, too!

I hope you and yours all have a lovely new year.

comments.title: Advice about killing a tree?
comments.content:
To JMG, or anyone else.

We have a giant old pin oak in our backyard. As trees do, it has grown--right into my neighbor's garage/workshop, which is now structurally damaged such that it will have to be scrapped. Neither neighbor nor I want anything to do with cutting down such a tree, but, for reasons that are beside the point of my question, it looks like it's going to have to be done. Neither of us can think of a way around it.

Best practices for treating the tree with dignity in this situation?

I realize you may advise me to talk to the tree about it. I've been doing that since I realized this was going to become a problem several years ago. I'm pretty sure the tree has tried to talk back, but I find myself to be about a C- student when it comes to sensing stuff like this. (I actually once asked a question to the tree and then watched as, immediately, an acorn dropped from its very top and plunked me between the eyes. I laughed out loud, because I had still had no idea what the tree wanted me to understand.) I have had a few dreams about the tree falling on its own very considerately between the two houses, which I've noted but don't know what to do with.

I've tried to cultivate a relationship with this tree, as well as with the garden and the other life in it (of which the oak is kind of the king), and the garden has responded by growing very happily in the last few years. I take that as a sign of trust. So I'm upset about the tree itself and also concerned about the impact on the rest of the garden and my relationship to it. So while I hope for a dream or a new idea, is there anything you'd recommend? Thanks as always.

Jonathan.

replies.title: Re: Advice about killing a tree?
replies.content: Well, if an acorn plunked me right between the eyes, I would take that as a broad hint to plant a new tree somewhere safe. As in "Take care of my babies, you. I mean YOU!"

replies.title: Re: Advice about killing a tree?
replies.content: Yes, in the end that's how I took that one. Thanks!

replies.title: Re: Advice about killing a tree?
replies.content: You could try to do something special with the wood from the tree. An oaken walking staff, wand, Yule log, hand-made spoon and bowl (using coals to create the hollows of the bowl and spoon, as Native Americans did to hollow out logs for making riverboats). Learn how to stack a cord of wood the prim, fussy New England way. Praise and/or memorialize the tree by creating a drawing, essay, poem/song or children's story based on it. Create a log (ha!) of all the information you can find about pin oaks: scientific name, growth habits, how old it was, medicinal uses, etc. It really depends how much work, time and emotional energy you want to invest.

replies.title: Re: Advice about killing a tree?
replies.content: Talk to the tree as soon as possible. Let it know what the situation is and what's going to happen. Let it know that you'll give it warning before the day comes, and ask it to let you know via dreams or intuition if there's anything it wants. Then follow through.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Making a journey in your imagination, typically using a magical symbol as a starting point. Let's say you want to work with the Tarot trumps. You take Trump 0, the Fool, and set it upright where you can look at it from a seated position. You enter into a meditative state, and imagine the image of the card expanding until it's the size of a door. Then you imagine the scene visible through it as though it's a three-dimensional scene -- there's the young man, there's the dog, and so on. Then you walk into the scene, pet the dog, and ask the young man to show you some of the secrets of Trump 0. You go on a journey with him, ending back where you began. You thank him, imagine yourself returning to your chair, imagine the card shrinking back to its normal size, and close. Then you write down everything that happened, because the things you encounter will often surprise you and even more often reveal things you didn't know, and you need to meditate on them to unpack what they have to teach. That's pathworking.

replies.title: Re: The nature of spirits
replies.content:
The world of spirits is far more complex than we have any way of understanding. The path we're on as human souls is only one of many paths that different classes of spirits take on their evolutionary journeys, so one size definitely does not fit all!

That said, the guardian spirits of species are considerably further along than we are, and are long past the equivalent of human existence. When they finish their work -- I suspect this happens when the species they are guarding and guiding finishes its time and goes extinct -- they go on to other things. At that level there's no need for death as we know it -- it's at the human level and below that the fresh start of death and rebirth is helpful -- but there's a transition.

Gods and goddesses are even further up the scale, and we're basically incapable of understanding what goes on at that level -- it's like expecting the dust mites in Mozart's wig to understand his musical career.

Faeries, djinn, and the like are other evolutions, following paths of their own.

And yes, when you finish your human lives, you'll be moving on to something more interesting, and at that point humans will call you "a spirit."

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hey JMG,

Wanted to ask your assessment on some comments I heard recently. I was listening to a podcast interview with Josephine McCarthy when she was discussing Quareia and some of her approach in magical training. One of the things she said, and I’ve heard her mention a few times now, is that the practice of banishing is not something which should be done regularly. In the first case, she says it can be disruptive to the spirits of place. Secondly, because everything is always banished, it can actually insulate a practitioner, such that they are stunted in their capacity to detect energies of spaces.

From what I can see, just glancing at the Quareia course, she places heavy emphasis on developing the ability to discern shifting energies in spaces, objects, and on the land, as being a key component in becoming a competent mage.

I wondered if you could speak to her thoughts. I wondered if her concern was more about the LBRP, which I know you’ve said functions differently than the SoP. Might her concerns be valid for the LBRP in a way that it isn’t with the SoP?

Thanks!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Well, obviously I disagree with her. To my mind regular banishing is as essential to spiritual hygiene as soap and water are to physical hygiene. If you only banish all the time that can lead to a certain amount of imbalance, which is why the LBRP is combined with the Middle Pillar exercise -- an invoking ritual -- and why the SoP includes invoking as well as banishing phases. But that's not the same thing as saying that banishing is a bad idea -- just that it should be balanced with invoking.

As for learning to sense energies, well, all I can say is that I've never encountered a problem with that, either in myself or in my students. Daily divination helps open the subtle senses; so does the regular practice of Pathworking; and if you need additional help, W.E. Butler's useful book How to Read the Aura, Practice Psychometry, Telepathy, and Clairvoyance -- by a student of Dion Fortune who as far as I know practiced the LBRP and Middle Pillar exercise every day of his mature life -- has plenty of useful exercises.

Still, your mileage may vary, and I'm sure McCarthy is as confident in her stance as I am in mine...

replies.title: Keeping Magically Clean
replies.content:
I write this with all respect to both JMG and Josephine McCarthy -I've studied and practiced the works of both- and they are both my most trusted sources on magic who are currently living & practicing.

While Josephine doesn't use banishings in her system there are a number of other things in place to keep the practitioner clean and spiritually/magically hygenic.

These include frequent salt water cleansing baths to clean the etheric & astral bodies. The use of scent of scents and sounds to tune a place/space and keep it clean. In place of the daily banishing she has the student practice a daily meditation that tunes them to the Void / Inner Flame. There are other practices -such as bathing in the Inner Flame in vision. Doing this has the effect of burning off accumulated gunk.

I don't bring this up to say one way is right and the other wrong. However I do think most magical schools will have some method for staying magically hygienic.

The choice of a magical school -or schools throughout a lifetime- is very personal. As JMG has pointed out here many times before, one size does not fit all, and what is good for one person could be detrimental to another.

One of the things I find wonderful in this world is the diversity of paths and religions. Raised as I was in a religion that taught it was the one and only true path became a sticking point for me that caused me to leave the church I was brought up in.

That's one of the best things the Druidry revival has going for it... It never claims to be the one true way, but is just one of many possible expressions of Divinity flowing out into this world.

A world with many different magical schools, training methods, and courses is the kind of world I like to see and encourage...

...and as JMG noted in a Magical Education, once you learn one system, then another, when you talk to people from other traditions you have the chance to compare notes.

I'll sign off with phrase one of my other magical teachers, Nema, used to say quite often, "Success to your work".

comments.title: Malaise
comments.content:
Hello again, JMG-
So, for the past several months, I've been practicing SOP and meditation. One of the things that seems to have been happening is that there have been a lot of days where I just sort of collapse into myself - it's a sort of combination of fatigue and boredom that makes me not want to go anywhere or do anything. I was wondering if this might somehow be a side of effect of the SOP (possibly me noticing things that I hadn't before), and if you had any suggestions as to how to combat this.
Thanks, and happy holidays!

replies.title: Re: Malaise
replies.content: That's a common form of what's called the Watcher at the Threshold -- in less melodramatic language, the efforts of the existing structures of your personality to resist the change that magical practice brings on. Push through it, keep doing your practices, and it'll go away. (Or as JRR Tolkien used to shout while driving a car straight through a busy intersection: "Charge 'em and they'll scatter!")

replies.title: Re: Malaise
replies.content:
I thought that was Teddy Roosevelt.

Or Genghis Khan.

replies.title: Re: Malaise
replies.content:
Not wanting to go anywhere or do anything can also be a symptom of Seasonal Affective Disorder, which is a fancy term for depression caused by lack of sunlight. I get a mild case of it every winter. Some people are helped by having a very bright light near them for about fifteen minutes in the morning, which mimics summer sunrise. I bought the cheapest form of such a light online for about thirty dollars. It's plugged in next to my computer, and I usually turn it on while I'm checking my email and blogs. It seems to be doing me some good.

If you live in a place with dark cold winters, it's likely that the body has a seasonal rhythm akin to circadian rhythms which prompts you to sleep more in the winter months. Expecting to have the same amount of energy and initiative every day of the year is really a demand of industrial society, with its 24 hour illumination, strict schedules and stimulants. It is unnatural. I don't think we should always fight the desire to stay home and take a nap, only if it makes us or other people unhappy.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Every presentation of that ritual -- and indeed, of nearly every ritual I've ever written about -- is different. That's partly to show the range of potential variation and partly to keep students from getting into the kind of mental rigor mortis typical of fundamentalism. Try the ritual both ways, choose the one that seems to work best for you, and meditate on the differences between them -- you'll learn far more that way.
